front: What is the definition of autocorrelation for a stationary time series?
back: Autocorrelation measures the linear correlation between a time series and a lagged version of itself. For a stationary series with lag k, it is defined as ρ_k = Cov(Y_t, Y_{t-k}) / Var(Y_t), which is the covariance divided by the variance.

front: What is the key property of the autocorrelation function (ACF) for any stationary model?
back: Two key properties are: 1) ρ_0 = 1 (correlation with itself at lag 0 is 1). 2) ρ_k = ρ_{-k} (the ACF is an even function, meaning the correlation is the same for positive and negative lags of the same magnitude).

front: How does the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) behave for a White Noise process?
back: For a White Noise process, all autocorrelations are zero for lags k > 0. Formally, ρ_k = 0 for all k ≠ 0. This is a fundamental property indicating no linear dependency between observations at different times.

front: What is the characteristic pattern of the ACF for an MA(q) (Moving Average) model?
back: The ACF for an MA(q) model has a distinct cutoff. It is non-zero only for lags k ≤ q and is zero for all lags k > q. This means the process has a memory of exactly q periods.

front: How does the Autocorrelation Function (ACF) typically behave for an AR(p) (Autoregressive) model?
back: The ACF for an AR(p) model decays gradually towards zero; it does not cut off abruptly. It can exhibit exponential decay or damped sinusoidal waves (oscillations), and the pattern depends on the roots of the characteristic equation.
